steps.m: to name, label, or mark something

steps.n: The root letters س-م-و become the noun 'name' by dropping the و and adding a pause mark (sukun) on م
Adding بِ at the start makes it mean 'with/by/in the name'
Adding the kasra at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added kasra at end to show possession/connection
Added 'the' to make it specific
Added vowels to connect the root letters together to make a basic past tense verb
The فَعْلان pattern adds intensity to the base meaning, indicating someone who possesses abundant mercy
Adding ال makes it 'the' specific merciful one rather than just any merciful one

steps.m: The Extremely Merciful One (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: The word gets a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end to show it belongs with or is connected to another word
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Forms intensive active participle meaning 'one who shows abundant mercy'
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'THE Ever Merciful'
The kasra at the end shows this word describes Allah in this context
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'praise'
Added ال at the beginning to mean 'the'
Added ُ at the end to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
This is the basic word for deity or god before any changes
Adding shadda on ل makes this the unique name for The One True God
Adding kasra at the end shows this belongs to or comes from Allah
Adding لِ at the start shows this praise belongs to or is for Allah. This word connects to الْحَمْدُ (praise) in the ayah to show who the praise is for.

steps.m: to care for and develop something to its full potential
master/lord (the one who perfectly cares for everything)
steps.n: The doubled letter (shown by ّ) makes the meaning stronger, showing complete and perfect care
The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah), showing that this lord belongs to or is possessed by Allah
Adding alif after first letter changes it from verb 'to know' into noun meaning 'world'
Added ين at the end to show there are many worlds
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific - these particular worlds, not just any worlds
Added َ at the end to show this word belongs to رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase رَبِّ ٱلْعٰلَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds)
Root letters ر-ح-م are connected with vowels to make the noun 'mercy'
Added ان to show someone who has this quality very strongly
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda for emphasis

steps.m: The Most Merciful (possessed by/connected to what follows)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added ي between ح and م with vowels to show someone who has a lot of mercy
Added ٱل at the start to make it specific, like saying 'the' in English
Added a shadda (ّ) on ر which doubles the letter to intensify the meaning of mercy
Added ِ at the end to show this word is describing الرَّحْمٰنِ that came before it

steps.m: to rule or have power over something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and changed middle vowel to kasra to show who does the action. This word is connected to يَوْمِ (day) in the phrase 'Owner of the Day of Judgment'
Changed alif to a longer alif (ٰ) to show stronger ownership and complete power
Added kasra at the end to show this word owns or possesses what comes after it (يَوْمِ - the Day)
Connected the root letters together with fatha and sukun to make the basic noun meaning 'day'
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to another word

steps.m: to judge, repay, follow a way

steps.n: The root letters get a long 'ee' sound (kasra) to change from an action (judging/repaying) into a system of beliefs and practices (religion)
The kasra at the end shows this word is affected by a preposition or being possessed
Adding ال makes it specific - we're talking about a particular religion

steps.m: specifically (used to emphasize who is being acted upon)
steps.n: This is a special word used to point directly to someone when making them the focus of attention

steps.m: You specifically (as the one being worshipped in نَعْبُدُ)
steps.n: Added كَ to show we're talking directly to someone. This word emphasizes that YOU are the one we worship, not anyone else.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the vowels to show this is happening now instead of in the past
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. This word is connected to إِيَّاكَ which means 'You alone' - together they mean 'You alone we worship'
This is a special word used to point directly to someone
Added كَ at the end to show we're talking to one person. This word is the object (receiver) of the verb نَعْبُدُ (we worship) in this ayah.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase. This word is also the object of the verb نَسْتَعِينُ (we seek help) in this ayah.
This root is about giving support to someone who needs it
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added letters at start to show seeking or requesting help from someone
Changed و to ا because it comes between two vowels
Changed to present tense 'we' form. This word shows we are seeking help from the one being addressed (إِيَّاكَ)
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he guided' to a command 'guide!' by adding alif at start and changing ending
Added نَا at the end to show who is being guided (us), and changed the initial alif to ٱ
Adding the basic vowels to make the word readable
Adding ا makes it the standard word for 'path'
Added ال to make it specific. The ص gets a shadda because it comes after ال
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb 'guide'. The alif is written as a small mark above.
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Added مُسْتَ at the start to mean 'seeking to be' straight - like asking for straightness
Changed vowel to ِ to show this describes someone/something that is straight
Added ٱلْ to make it definite. This word describes 'the path' (ٱلصِّرٰطَ) in this ayah
Adding the basic vowels to make it readable
The alif with a line over it (ٰ) is just a different way of writing the same word - it doesn't change the meaning. Both spellings mean 'path' and can refer to a straight path.

steps.m: the straight path (object of اهدنا/guide us)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb اهدنا (guide us) from the previous verse
This is the basic form of the word that points to someone or something
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ letter to make it specific
Changed the ending to ين to show we're talking about multiple people. This word describes the people who received Allah's favor (أَنْعَمْتَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to actively give blessing to someone' instead of just 'to bless'
Adding تَ at the end changes it from 'he' to 'you'. This word connects to عَلَيْهِمْ (upon them) in the ayah to show who received the blessing.
This is a basic preposition showing position or relationship. It's one of the most common Arabic words that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
The ى changes to ي when adding pronouns at the end. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: on/upon them (refers to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them' (masculine plural). This refers back to الَّذِينَ (those who) mentioned earlier in the ayah.

steps.m: to be other than or different from

steps.n: Basic noun form showing something different or opposite
The word is now in genitive case, which often happens after certain prepositions
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb

steps.m: one who is made angry at
steps.n: Changed to passive participle form showing who receives the anger

steps.m: the one who is made angry at
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific (this refers to those who earned Allah's anger)

steps.m: the one who is made angry at (object of عَلَيْهِمْ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word follows عَلَيْهِمْ (upon them)
This is a basic preposition showing position or relationship. It's not derived from a root - it's a fundamental word.
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching pronouns. This is just a spelling change, not a meaning change.

steps.m: upon them (referring to ٱلَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Adding هِمْ shows this applies to a group of people. In this ayah, it refers back to 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) that were favored.
This is a basic negative particle that makes something 'not' true. Here it negates 'those who go astray' (ٱلضَّآلِّينَ) in the ayah.

steps.m: and not (connects to غَيْرِ and negates ٱلضَّآلِّينَ)
steps.n: Adding وَ at the start connects this word to غَيْرِ (other than) that came before it, adding the meaning 'and'. The fatha marks (َ) are added to help with pronunciation.

steps.m: to wander off the right path
steps.n: This describes someone who has lost their way or gone off the right path
Adding the vowel marks makes this a past tense verb meaning someone actually went astray

steps.m: one who wanders off the right path
steps.n: Changed from a past-tense verb (he went astray) to a noun describing someone who goes astray, by adding alif and doubling the last letter

steps.m: the one who wanders off the right path
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the one who persistently wanders off the right path
steps.n: Changed alif to long alif to show this is a lasting quality

steps.m: those who persistently wander off the right path
steps.n: Added ين to show this refers to many people
